label: no
cot_prefix: Okay, so I need to figure out if an 8×8 chessboard with two diagonally opposite corners removed can be completely covered by 31 dominoes. Each domino covers two adjacent squares. Let me think about this step by step. First, I remember that a regular 8×8 chessboard has 64 squares. If we remove two corners that are diagonally opposite, that leaves us with 62 squares. Since each domino covers two squares, 31 dominoes would indeed cover all 62 squares. But the question is whether such a tiling is possible. I recall that domino tiling problems often involve colorings of the chessboard. A standard chessboard is colored with alternating black and white squares. Each domino, when placed on the board, will always cover one black and one white square. So, if the number of black and white squares is equal, then maybe a tiling is possible. Let me check the color of the two removed corners. The chessboard has an equal number of black and white squares. Each corner square is of the same color as the squares diagonal to them. For example, if the bottom-left corner is black, then the top-right corner is also black because they are diagonally opposite. Similarly, the other two corners (top-left and bottom-right) would be white. So, if we remove two diagonally opposite corners, say both black squares, then the remaining board would have 30 black squares and 32 white squares. Because we started with 32 black and 32 white, and removed two black ones. But each domino must cover one black and one white. Therefore, if we have an imbalance in the number of black and white squares, we can't tile the board perfectly. Because each domino accounts for one of each. So if there are 30 black and 32 white, we would need 30 dominoes to cover the 30 black squares, but those 30 dominoes would only cover 30 white squares, leaving 2 white squares uncovered. Hence, the tiling is impossible. Wait, let me verify again. Let me count the squares. Original chessboard: 8x8=64. Two corners removed: 62. Each domino covers 2 squares. 62/2=31 dominoes.
